annotations.text: Did this patient have a community-acquired pneumonia? The increased frequency of community-acquired pneumonias is well recognized in patients infected with HIV. The most common pathogens are  *Streptococcus pneumoniae* and  *Haemophilus influenzae* , but other pathogens, such as  *Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Staphylococcus aureus, Branhamella catarrhalis, Rhodococcus equi* , and group B streptococcus, have also been identified with increased frequen

Since  *Pneumocystis carinii* is the most common single pathogen causing pneumonia in patients with AIDS, occurring in 80 percent of such patients during the course of their illness, I have to include it in the differential diagnosis of this patient's disease
Another opportunistic pathogen frequently encountered in association with  *P. carinii* in lung tissue of patients with AIDS is cytomegalovirus

There are well-documented cases of pneumonia in the AIDS population that has been attributed to  *Toxoplasma gondii* and lung involvement with this unicellular protozoan parasite has frequently been found in patients who have died of toxoplasmosis

*Strongyloides stercoralis* is another interesting protozoan. Infection with  *S. stercoralis* is among the opportunistic infections considered indicative of an underlying cell-mediated immunodeficiency in patients with AIDS

If I am correct and this is a case of histoplasmosis, however, it is possible that the two episodes were related since the latency period between the onset of infection and the time of the diagnosis can be as long as six to eight months in this disease.

Although I cannot rule out miliary tuberculosis on the basis of the findings, I favor progressive disseminated histoplasmosis as the principal diagnosis in this case because of the history of recent previous residence in an area where the infection is endemic and the relatively slow evolution of the disease, with minimal respiratory tract symptoms.

The lung is affected by one of these processes as the initial manifestation of AIDS in about 65 percent of patients infected with HIV. Clinically important pulmonary pathologic changes develop in over 80 percent of these patients.

Anemia is the most common hematologic disturbance in cases of disseminated histoplasmosis. Since histoplasma invades the reticuloendothelial system, one can postulate an impaired release of iron derived from the catabolism of hemoglobin, with a subsequent reduction in supply to the marrow.

The nonproductive cough, dyspnea, and fever, the widened arterialalveolar gradient, and the radiographic pattern of diffuse interstitial pulmonary infiltrates are all consistent with pneumonia caused by  *P. carinii.* Several features of the case, however, are inconsistent with this diagnosis

Fever and dyspnea are frequent in patients with pulmonary toxoplasmosis, but cough and sputum production are usually absent. A maculopapular rash may occur. Bilateral reticulonodular infiltrates can be seen on radiographs of the chest, but the findings are more often confined to one or two lobes.

the miliary pattern seen on the radiographs of the chest is very suggestive of tuberculosis. However, the lack of positive smears on multiple specimens, the absence of obvious organ involvement outside the lungs, and the relatively slow evolution of the diffuse pulmonary infiltrates make a diagnosis of miliary tuberculosis less likely.
